book.title: The Architecture of Silence
book.author: Emad Jumaah
book.dedication: For my father, who taught me to read slowly.

chapter.title: The City Without Rain
chapter.epigraph: We build in stone what we fear to say in words.
chapter.openingParagraph: The city had no memory of rain. Not in the way that cities forget things — gradually, gracefully — but in the abrupt manner of a mind that has decided forgetting is cleaner than remembering.
chapter.body1: She walked the corridors at dawn, when the marble floors still held the cool of the night and the air had not yet thickened with the day's ambition.
chapter.body2: The archive was older than the building that housed it. That was the first thing they told you, and the last thing you remembered.
chapter.body3: The earliest entry was dated not by year but by season. Autumn, it said. The ink had faded to the colour of old tea.
chapter.body4: She photographed each page. Not because she doubted her memory, but because she had learned to distrust her interpretations of it.
chapter.pullquote: The past is not a place you visit. It is a place that visits you.
chapter.body5: Three months later, the building was closed for renovation.
chapter.body6: She never returned. Some doors, once shut, resist the key not because they are locked but because they have decided to remain closed.
chapter.closingParagraph: The city continued without rain. And she continued without the archive. Both managed.
chapter.footnote1: The archive on Al-Rayyan Road was established in 1947. Its catalogue was last updated in 2019.
chapter.footnote2: Interview conducted March 2026. Name withheld by request.
